title: Lucas-Kanade vs. Farneback Methods
concept: Lucas-Kanade computes sparse optical flow for selected keypoints, while the Farneback method computes dense optical flow for all pixels.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the math formula assumption for brightness constancy?

options.text: Intensity(x, y, t) = Intensity(x + u, y + v, t + 1)

exercises.explain: Yes! The intensity of the pixel must remain constant as it moves to its new location over a time step.

cards.title: Detail vs. Speed
cards.body: When you watch a video of a busy street, do you need to know where every single particle of dust is flying? Or do you only care about tracking the cars? In classical computer vision, we choose between tracking a few keypoints (fast) or tracking every pixel (detailed).

cards.title: Sparse vs. Dense Flow
cards.body:
• Lucas-Kanade is a sparse optical flow method. It only calculates movement vectors for a selected set of easy-to-track keypoints (like corners).
• Farneback is a dense optical flow method. It calculates a motion vector for every single pixel in the frame.

cards.title: Tracking pins vs. a rubber sheet
cards.body: Sparse flow is like sticking pins into a few landmark points on a moving shirt and tracking only the pins. Dense flow is like tracking the stretch and folding of every single thread in the fabric across the entire shirt.

cards.title: Security camera tracking
cards.body: For a security system identifying vehicle speeds: Lucas-Kanade tracks the license plate corners (sparse). For an autonomous vehicle mapping the entire shape of an obstacles, Farneback calculates the dense motion field of the entire road.

cards.title: Dense isn't always best
cards.body: You might think dense flow is always better because it provides more detail. But dense flow is highly expensive! Calculating vectors for millions of pixels every frame can easily slow your model down, whereas sparse flow can run in real-time on basic processors.

left.text:
Tracks select keypoints only
Extremely fast computational speed
Provides sparse coordinate vectors

right.text:
Tracks every pixel in the frame
Slow and computationally heavy
Provides a full flow matrix image

exercises.prompt: Which optical flow method calculates motion vectors for every single pixel?
exercises.explain: Correct! Dense methods like Farneback calculate flow vectors for all pixels, not just keypoints.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Lucas-Kanade is generally faster to compute than Farneback's method.
exercises.explain: True. Because Lucas-Kanade only processes a sparse subset of keypoints, it requires much less math.

exercises.sentence: If you only need to track the movement of a few distinct corner points, you should use the ___ method.
exercises.explain: Lucas-Kanade is the standard sparse method for tracking keypoints (like corners).

exercises.prompt: Which application requires a dense optical flow method like Farneback?

options.text: A video editor calculating pixel warping for digital slow-motion
A basic counter tracking cars passing a highway camera line
A simple camera stabilization script tracking 10 points

exercises.explain: Generating slow-motion frames requires warping the entire image grid, which demands a dense vector for every pixel.

exercises.prompt: You are building a motion tracking system for a low-power drone. The drone must run in real-time at 60 FPS on a tiny battery. Which method is most appropriate?

options.text: Lucas-Kanade, because it is lightweight and can run on limited hardware by only tracking keypoints.
Farneback, because drones require pixel-perfect vectors for every leaf on the trees.
Decompressing the MP4 file manually using 3D convolutions on the CPU.
None, drones must use static image classification instead.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The low-power drone needs to save CPU cycles. Lucas-Kanade's sparse tracking is perfect for keeping the calculation fast. Next: how does deep learning take over optical flow? Next up: FlowNet!
